primary_diagnosis.name: Acute limb ischaemia, right upper limb (suspected cardioembolic)

pathophysiology_summary: In a patient with known atrial fibrillation and recently missed anticoagulant doses, stasis of blood in the fibrillating left atrium (particularly the atrial appendage) predisposes to thrombus formation; a fragment can embolise distally and acutely occlude the brachial artery. Sudden cessation of arterial inflow produces the classic '6 Ps': Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paraesthesia, Perishing cold, and Paralysis, evolving over minutes to hours. Skeletal muscle and peripheral nerve tissue are exquisitely sensitive to ischaemia, with irreversible necrosis typically beginning within 4-6 hours of complete occlusion. This is a limb-threatening (and via the same embolic mechanism, potentially life-threatening if the clot showers to cerebral or coronary circulation) emergency requiring emergency embolectomy or thrombolysis; via telehealth, absent pulses cannot be palpated, but the video-observable pallor/mottling plus progressive paraesthesia/weakness and cold sensation are sufficient historical/observational evidence to mandate immediate ambulance dispatch to a centre with vascular surgery capability.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe pain with numbness and coldness in a single limb.
Can cause arm pain and paraesthesia, though the described pallor and coldness are not features of a nerve root process.
Causes acute arm symptoms, though DVT typically produces a warm, swollen, erythematous limb rather than pallor and coldness.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Sudden unilateral pain with pallor and coldness should already raise acute limb ischaemia as leading even before the AF history is elicited.

differential.diagnosis: Acute limb ischaemia (suspected cardioembolic, secondary to AF with missed anticoagulation)
differential.evidence_basis: Video-observed pallor/mottling (DI-001), progressive paraesthesia/weakness (DI-002), known AF with 3 missed anticoagulant doses (DI-003), and perishing cold with a demarcation line (DI-004) together confirm a high-probability embolic arterial occlusion.
Pallor, coldness, and a temperature demarcation line are vascular, not neurological, findings and are inconsistent with radiculopathy.
Pallor and coldness (rather than warmth, swelling, and redness) argue strongly against DVT.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage acute limb ischaemia with a cardioembolic source should be the sole leading diagnosis, mandating immediate emergency ambulance dispatch to a vascular surgery centre.

differential.diagnosis: Acute limb ischaemia, right upper limb (suspected cardioembolic)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires examination (absent pulses) and arterial duplex/CT angiography, unavailable remotely. Emergency escalation to a vascular surgery centre is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A management plan without immediate 000 dispatch at this stage represents a critical failure with a narrow limb-salvage window.

red_flags.description: Video-observed pallor and early mottling of the hand/forearm
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pallor and mottling reflect critical reduction in arterial perfusion; a video-observable proxy for the 'Pallor' component of the 6 Ps obtainable without physical examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ischaemia progresses undetected toward irreversible tissue necrosis.

red_flags.description: Progressive paraesthesia evolving to numbness and new motor weakness/loss of grip
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sensory then motor loss ('Paraesthesia' and 'Paralysis' of the 6 Ps) indicates advancing nerve and muscle ischaemia and signals the closing salvage window.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to irreversible neuromuscular necrosis and permanent loss of function.

red_flags.description: Known atrial fibrillation with 3 days of missed anticoagulant doses
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies the likely cardioembolic source and explains the acute event; also raises concern for showering emboli to other territories (stroke, mesenteric, coronary).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Underlying cause and risk of further embolic events not recognised or communicated to the receiving team.

red_flags.description: Absent radial, ulnar, and brachial pulses with a temperature demarcation line at the mid-forearm (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms complete arterial occlusion and localises its level; requires palpation unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — escalation must rely on the video-observable and historical features above.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Delay to solve the transport problem
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient living alone and unable to drive might tempt an AI Doctor to spend time problem-solving alternative transport (rideshare, neighbour) rather than immediately directing an ambulance call — in acute limb ischaemia, any delay directly costs viable tissue.

Failing to screen for and safety-net against concurrent embolisation to other territories (sudden chest pain, facial droop/speech difficulty, abdominal pain) in a patient with a likely intracardiac thrombus source.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Precise temperature demarcation line on examination
Arterial duplex ultrasound or CT angiography
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000), Priority 1 dispatch to a centre with vascular surgery capability for urgent embolectomy/thrombolysis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Any delay — advising the patient to wait, apply heat, elevate the arm, or arrange non-emergency transport — risks irreversible tissue necrosis and limb loss within the narrow salvage window, and does not address the risk of concurrent embolisation elsewhere.
